cases.title: Vomiting Unresponsive Patient
cases.presentation: An unresponsive 50-year-old ejected from a vehicle suddenly vomits. You hear loud gurgling. Your partner is maintaining in-line cervical stabilization.
cases.question: What is the immediate priority?
options: Suction with a rigid tip, then ventilate with BVM and oxygen
cases.explanation: Active vomiting and gurgling require immediate suctioning with a rigid (Yankauer) tip to protect the airway from aspiration, followed by assisted ventilation with BVM and oxygen. Definitive airway management follows once the airway is clear.
cases.clinicalPearl: Keep a working rigid suction within arm's reach for every obtunded trauma patient—aspiration can occur in seconds.

cases.title: Failed Intubation, Rescue Plan
cases.presentation: You are managing a difficult trauma airway. After one intubation attempt the SpO2 falls from 96% to 84% and you could not visualize the cords.
cases.question: What is the best next action?
options: Stop, ventilate with BVM to reoxygenate, then place a supraglottic rescue airway
Perform a surgical airway right away
cases.explanation: When an attempt fails and the patient desaturates, the priority is reoxygenation by BVM. A supraglottic (BIAD) rescue airway is the appropriate next device. A surgical airway is reserved for true cannot-intubate, cannot-ventilate situations.
cases.clinicalPearl: Oxygenation, not tube placement, is the goal. Reoxygenate between attempts and move to a rescue device early.

cases.title: Confirming the Tube After a Move
cases.presentation: You intubated a crash patient on the ground and confirmed placement with capnography. After lifting him onto the stretcher and into the ambulance, the capnography waveform suddenly flattens and breath sounds are now absent.
cases.question: What is the most likely problem and action?
options: Tube dislodgement during the move — reassess placement and re-intubate or rescue-ventilate
cases.explanation: A sudden loss of waveform and breath sounds after moving the patient signals tube dislodgement (a leading cause of airway disaster). Reassess immediately: visualize/re-confirm, re-intubate, or ventilate with BVM/BIAD while reoxygenating.
cases.clinicalPearl: Reconfirm tube placement with capnography after every move—the flat waveform is your early-warning system.
